HFO.ai_summary: Showers are expected to persist throughout the day over Kauai and Oahu with the highest precipitation rates concentrated over Oahu. Light precipitation changes are anticipated toward the end of the forecast period. The current atmospheric conditions indicate a state of limited instability characterized by warm, saturated air and a poor lapse rate.

PIH.discussion_raw:
119
FXUS65 KPIH 121825
AFDPIH

Area Forecast Discussion
National Weather Service Pocatello ID
1225 PM MDT Sun Apr 12 2026

.KEY MESSAGES...

- Strong to severe storms again this afternoon with gusts up to
  60 mph and small hail the main threats.

- Mountain snow on and off through Monday with isolated to
  scattered valley rain showers.

- Seasonable temperatures through Wednesday before next system
  arrives bringing more precipitation and even cooler
  temperatures for Thursday and Friday.

&&

.DISCUSSION...
Issued at 1221 PM MDT Sun Apr 12 2026

Another round of showers and thunderstorms is on tap for this
afternoon and evening as the low we`ve been talking about for
several days now continues to slowly make its way through northern
California/eastern Oregon and into central Idaho. We`re already
starting to see things light up on radar this afternoon with very
pulse-y thunderstorms popping up. These storms today will once again
have the potential to produce strong to severe gusts to around 60
mph with small hail as all of Eastern Idaho is highlighted in SPC`s
"Marginal" risk today. Surface CAPE isn`t quite as strong today,
with the HREF ensemble generally showing ranges from 200-400 J/kg
across the area, but more clearing/heating than what we say
yesterday it will improve overall instability compared to yesterday.
It will also be windy outside of storms, too, with gusts of 35-45
mph for many. The cold front associated with this system will be
through by Monday, so highs on Monday afternoon will be cooler, only
making it into the low to mid 50s for most. We continue to see
showers into Monday, especially in the Central Mountains where snow
levels will fall to near 5000-5500 ft for Monday which means more
mountains snow ahead. Around Stanley, the forecast shows about 1 to
3 inches of snow while Galena Summit looks closer to 2 to 5 inches
of snow from this afternoon to tomorrow night. Snow totals will be a
bit lighter in the Eastern Highlands, less than an inch on Pine
Creek Pass and closer to 2 to 4 inches for Emigration summit.

By Tuesday, this system will be departing and we will get a bit of a
break in the activity, though a few isolated showers could linger in
the Eastern Highlands into the afternoon. As soon as early Wednesday
morning, our next system begins to move into the Central Mountains
and will spread eastward throughout the rest of our CWA during the
day. Wednesday will be a windy day, especially in the upper Snake
River Plain where gusts are already forecast to be pushing 40-45
mph. Thursday will also be breezy with widespread gusts of 30-40
mph. This system looks to bring in decent moisture for everyone and
colder temperatures as highs will go from mid to upper 50s on
Wednesday to low to mid 40s on Thursday. With temperatures dropping
and precipitation continuing, we are expecting more snowfall in the
mid to upper elevations, but there will also be some potential for
light snow down to valley floors early Thursday morning and early
Friday morning. Of course, this is several days away, so we will
watch this closely over the next few days.

&&

.AVIATION /18Z TAFS THROUGH 18Z MONDAY/...
Issued at 1139 AM MDT Sun Apr 12 2026

Another round of convection is expected this afternoon and
evening with PROB30 TSRA at each East Idaho terminal. VFR
conditions continue, though stronger storms could produce
temporary CIG restrictions to MVFR. Storms will be capable of
producing 45-50kt outflow winds once again, but the general wind
pattern will also support wind gusts outside thunderstorm
activity reaching 25-30kts. Expect dry conditions overnight for
BYI, PIH, and IDA, though SHRA/SHSN will be possible late
tonight into Monday morning for SUN and DIJ. Another round of
more consolidated SHRA reaches SUN for Monday afternoon,
spreading east but decreasing in coverage for the remainder of
the terminals.

&&

.PIH WATCHES/WARNINGS/ADVISORIES...
None.

&&

$$

DISCUSSION...AMM
AVIATION...DMH

PIH.ai_summary:
Showers and thunderstorms are expected this afternoon and evening as a low-pressure system moves through northern California, eastern Oregon, and central Idaho, producing pulse-y convection with potential for strong wind gusts up to 60 mph and small hail across Eastern Idaho. Surface CAPE is not exceptionally strong but instability is expected to improve compared to the previous day. A cold front associated with this system will pass by Monday, leading to cooler temperatures in the mountains on Monday afternoon. Snow levels are forecast to be near 5000 to 5500 feet in the Central Mountains on Monday, indicating snow potential at higher elevations, with specific areas expecting between one and five inches of snow.

By Tuesday, the system will depart, allowing for a break in activity, though isolated showers may remain in the Eastern Highlands. A subsequent system is anticipated to move into the Central Mountains early Wednesday morning, spreading eastward throughout the region. Wednesday will be windy, particularly in the upper Snake River Plain with gusts up to 40 to 45 mph. Thursday will also be breezy with widespread gusts of 30 to 40 mph, bringing in moisture and cooler temperatures, with highs dropping from the mid to upper 50s on Wednesday to the low to mid 40s on Thursday. This progression is expected to bring increased snowfall at mid to upper elevations, with potential for light snow down to valley floors early Thursday morning and early Friday morning.

BOI.discussion_raw:
351
FXUS65 KBOI 121740
AFDBOI

Area Forecast Discussion
National Weather Service Boise ID
1140 AM MDT Sun Apr 12 2026

.UPDATE...Updated aviation discussion.

&&

.KEY MESSAGES...
- Isolated afternoon thunderstorms over southwest Idaho today
  with gusty outflow winds.

- Slightly below normal temperatures with valley rain showers
  and mountain snow showers today through tomorrow morning.

- Much cooler temperatures with periods of precipitation
  Wednesday through Friday.

&&

.SHORT TERM /Through Tuesday Night/...
Issued 313 AM MDT SUN APR 12 2026
Widespread precipitation is filling in south of the Snake River
Valley as of 3AM MDT. This is the first push of precipitation
along the outer band of the coastal low currently moving inland
over northern California. This will push northward throughout
the morning, bringing widespread rain below about 7000 feet MSL.
Much cooler air will move in behind this initial push of
precipitation later today. Temperatures will drop about 5-10
degrees with this cooler air, with snow levels dropping to
5500-6500 feet MSL by mid-morning.

Weak instability exists mainly over high terrain in central
Idaho down to the Magic Valley and near the ID-NV border. This
will support a slight chance of thunderstorms late this morning
through the early evening. Some storms could be strong with
gusty outflow winds up to 50 mph thanks to elevated winds aloft
with the incoming low. However, the best conditions for any of
these stronger thunderstorms is over Eastern Idaho.

As the low moves east of southern Idaho late Sunday, wraparound
moisture and cooler northerly flow aloft will push a secondary
band of precipitation into the region early Monday. This will
bring much lower snow levels (4500-5500 feet MSL) and
widespread precipitation to the region through Monday morning.
Mountain valleys could see 1-3 inches of snow, mountain passes
on US95 and ID21 could see up to 6 inches of snow, and high
peaks over 8000 feet could see up to 12 inches of snow by Monday
morning. Liquid totals from Sunday and Monday`s periods of
precipitation will reach 0.50-1.0 inches of rain north of the
Treasure Valley, with locally higher amounts over high terrain.
Areas near the Nevada border will reach 0.10-0.25 inches of
rain. Precipitation will taper off Monday evening as a brief
dry period moves in on Tuesday.

&&

.LONG TERM /Wednesday through Sunday/...
Issued 313 AM MDT SUN APR 12 2026
Precipitation will return Wednesday, after Tuesday`s brief
lull. As a low digs down the coast of British Columbia,
widespread precipitation will set in across the area along a
cold front. Precipitation will linger through Thursday as the
main low moves over our area. Temperatures on Wednesday will be
around 10 degrees below normal, cooling to 10-15 degrees below
normal Thursday following the cold front. Rain won`t be the
only precip type in play with this system. The 500mb heights are
in the 5-15th percentile of climatology will support lowering
snow levels, from 4.5-6.5 kft Wednesday afternoon to valleys
floors come Thursday. Breezy winds will also accompany this
system Wednesday/Thursday. Over the course of Wednesday through
Thursday, liquid precipitation totals of 0.15-0.50 inches are
expected throughout E-Oregon and within the Snake Plain, with
0.6-1.5 inches over the Boise/West Central Mountain zones and
higher peaks elsewhere. Regarding snowfall, mountains will
receive 8-14 inches by Thursday morning (1-4 inches in higher
elevation mountain valleys), locations down to 4500 ft MSL will
generally see under an inch of snow by Thursday morning. Through
Thursday afternoon, locations down to 4500 ft can see
additional amounts up to an inch, with 1-3 inches of additional
snowfall over the West Central Mountains. Treasure Valley sites
could also see snow flurries Thursday, although little to no
accumulation is expected.

A warming and drying trend will set in beyond Thursday, as the
system moves east and ridging builds in across the area.
Temperatures will return to near normal come Saturday, warming
even more Sunday. The dry conditions don`t appear to last too
long, as another system will impact the area come early next
week.

&&

.AVIATION /18Z Sunday through Monday/...
Issued 1135 AM MDT SUN APR 12 2026
Widespread rain showers continue today. Isolated thunderstorms
across the area could produce heavy rain and 25 mph outflows.
A strong frontal passage in the Magic Valley will bring gusts up
to 45 mph this afternoon. Precipitation moves north briefly
tonight, before moving back south tomorrow morning. Surface
winds: becoming S-W 10-20 kt with gusts to 20-30 kt. Winds
aloft at 10kft MSL: SW 20-35 kt

KBOI...Rain showers through the day bring MVFR conditions. There
is a 10% chance of thunder this afternoon, which may bring gusty
winds. Then a drier period overnight before more rain tomorrow
morning. Surface winds variable in showers 5-15 kt, stray gusts
up to 25 kt from distant storms are possible this afternoon.

&&

.BOI WATCHES/WARNINGS/ADVISORIES...
ID...None.

OR...None.

&&

$$

www.weather.gov/Boise

www.facebook.com/NWSBoise
www.x.com/NWSBoise

AVIATION.....JM
SHORT TERM...SA
LONG TERM....NF

BOI.ai_summary:
Widespread precipitation is expected to fill in south of the Snake River Valley as a result of the outer band of a coastal low moving inland, bringing rain throughout the morning. Cooler air will advance behind this initial precipitation, causing temperatures to drop significantly and snow levels to descend to 5500-6500 feet MSL by mid-morning. Weak instability is present over high terrain in central Idaho, which may support isolated thunderstorms with gusty outflow winds, particularly when elevated winds aloft are present from the approaching low. As the low moves east of southern Idaho late Sunday, wraparound moisture and cooler northerly flow aloft will introduce a secondary band of precipitation early Monday, resulting in much lower snow levels and widespread precipitation through Monday morning, with mountain valleys potentially receiving several inches of snow.

From Wednesday through Thursday, a system associated with a low digging down the coast of British Columbia will bring widespread precipitation along a cold front. Temperatures will be significantly below normal, cooling to 10-15 degrees below normal on Wednesday and further on Thursday. This system is expected to support lowering snow levels, with valleys experiencing snow floors by Thursday morning, and liquid precipitation totals are forecast across Eastern Oregon and the Snake Plain. Mountain areas are projected to receive substantial snowfall, with higher peaks seeing up to twelve inches by Monday morning, and additional snowfall expected over the West Central Mountains through Thursday afternoon. Following this period, a warming and drying trend will establish as ridging builds in.

REV.discussion_raw:
001
FXUS65 KREV 120836
AFDREV

Area Forecast Discussion
National Weather Service Reno NV
136 AM PDT Sun Apr 12 2026

.KEY MESSAGES...

* Colder today with rain and mountain snow producing winter travel
  impacts for the eastern Sierra.

* A slight 10-20% chance for additional thunderstorms continue
  through the afternoon.

* Periods of gusty winds this afternoon for western NV, eastern
  Lassen County, and east of the crests in Mono County.

&&

.DISCUSSION...

* As of 1:30 AM PDT, heavy snow continues in the eastern Sierra
  with rates of around 2"/hour. Rain along with snow to as low as
  5,000 ft is also observed in western NV. Per latest high-
  resolution model guidance, the primary band of precipitation
  will exit the Tahoe Basin by around 3-5 AM, then eventually Mono
  County by around 5-7 AM. Spillover precipitation will continue
  across the Reno-Carson City-Minden areas through around 4-5 AM,
  and the inner-basin and ranges through 7-8 AM.

* Snow showers will likely (>70%) persist all day in the Sierra,
  with likely (60%) rates around 0.5-1"/hour. With the main cold
  low moving overhead, instability will increase this afternoon.
  Latest HREF/Hi-Res models show lapse rates of 7-8C/km advecting
  into the region, with SBCAPES in the Sacramento/northern San
  Joaquin Valleys of ~250-500 J/kg. As such, we can`t rule out
  snowfall rates up to 2-3"/hour in heavier convective
  thunderstorms that move into the Sierra this afternoon. There is
  also a low (20%) chance for lightning today. We`re looking at
  additional snow totals of 12-14", with locally up to 18" along
  the highest peaks in heavier snow showers.

* For western NV, eastern Lassen County, and Mono County east of
  the crests, the HREF and high-resolution models show that today
  will be mostly precipitation shadowed, with a slight 20-30%
  chance of showers. Southwest winds will increase this afternoon
  in these aforementioned areas, with gusts up to 35-45 mph.

* A majority of the precipitation comes to an end later this
  evening. But, there remains a 30-50% chance for leftover showers
  through Monday afternoon in the Sierra primarily from the Tahoe
  Basin south into Mono County.

* We`ll see a brief warmup Tuesday and Wednesday with highs near
  mid-April averages before another fast moving storm drops into
  the region. We can expect increasing winds, valley rain and
  mountain snow showers, followed by cooler than average
  temperatures through late next week. -McKellar

&&

.AVIATION...

* FL100 S-SW wind gusts to 50-60 kt today, resulting in widespread
  turbulence and periods of LLWS especially through tonight.
  Surface wind gusts at main terminals near 30 kt this afternoon.

* Snow rates of 0.5-1"/hour, producing IFR/LIFR conditions, with
  spotty additional accumulations of 1-4" for Sierra terminals. We
  cannot rule out heavier snow rates of 2-3"/hour in convective
  snow showers.

* Western NV terminals will primarily be rain, but could see some
  snow mix with rain this morning, producing MVFR/brief IFR
  conditions. Chances for any runway snow accumulation are less
  than 10% at KRNO/KCXP/KMEV. Isolated rain/snow/pellet showers
  return this afternoon-early evening with brief MVFR periods.
  -McKellar

&&

.AVALANCHE...

Moderate to heavy snow will continue to impact all avalanche center
terrain through Sunday evening.

* Snow totals, levels, and rates: Additional snow totals of 12-14",
  with locally up to 18" along the highest peaks in BAC terrain.
  Snow levels as of this writing are around 5000 ft, but are
  expected to rise to ~5500 ft this afternoon. Snowfall rates peak
  early this morning with rates of 2-3"/hr until about sunrise.
  Otherwise, rates of 0.5-1.5"/hr (highest for SAC terrain). Any
  convective-type snow bands that form could have rates up to
  2-3"/hr.

* SLRs and SWE: SLRs of 11-13:1 today, locally up to 15:1.
  Additional SWE (50th percentile) of 1-1.5", locally lower of
  0.75-1.25" for ESAC terrain.

* Thunder potential and wind gusts: 20-25% chance of thunder along
  the crest today, mainly this afternoon. Southwest wind gusts of 60-
  80 mph today.

-Giralte

&&

.REV Watches/Warnings/Advisories...
NV...Winter Storm Warning until 11 PM PDT Sunday NVZ002.

CA...Winter Weather Advisory until 11 PM PDT Sunday CAZ071.

     Winter Storm Warning until 11 PM PDT Sunday CAZ072-073.

&&

$$

* Snow totals, levels, and rates: Additional snow totals of 12-14",

  convective-type snow bands that form could have rates up to

* SLRs and SWE: SLRs of 11-13:1 today, locally up to 15:1.
  Additional SWE (50th percentile) of 1-1.5", locally lower of
  0.75-1.25" for ESAC terrain.

* Thunder potential and wind gusts: 20-25% chance of thunder along

  80 mph today.

-Giralte

&&

.REV Watches/Warnings/Advisories...
NV...Winter Storm Warning until 11 PM PDT Sunday NVZ002.

CA...Winter Weather Advisory until 11 PM PDT Sunday CAZ071.

     Winter Storm Warning until 11 PM PDT Sunday CAZ072-073.

&&

REV.ai_summary:
Heavy snow continues in the eastern Sierra with rates around two inches per hour, accompanied by rain and snow down to five thousand feet across western Nevada. The primary band of precipitation is expected to exit the Tahoe Basin by three to five AM, followed by Mono County by five to seven AM, with spillover affecting the Reno-Carson City-Minden areas through four to five AM and the inner-basin and ranges through seven to eight AM. Snow showers are likely to persist throughout the day in the Sierra with rates around half an inch to one inch per hour, and instability is expected to increase this afternoon as a low moves overhead. Models indicate lapse rates of seven to eight degrees Celsius per kilometer advecting into the region, with potential snowfall rates up to two to three inches per hour in heavier convective thunderstorms moving into the Sierra this afternoon, leading to potential totals of twelve to fourteen inches, with local peaks reaching eighteen inches along the highest elevations.

For western Nevada, eastern Lassen County, and Mono County east of the crests, the area will experience mostly precipitation shadowing with a slight chance of showers, and southwest winds will increase this afternoon with gusts up to thirty-five to forty-five miles per hour. A majority of the precipitation is forecast to cease later this evening, though there remains a thirty to fifty percent chance for leftover showers through Monday afternoon in the Sierra, primarily from the Tahoe Basin south into Mono County. Moderate to heavy snow will continue to impact all avalanche center terrain through Sunday evening, with snow levels expected to rise to approximately five thousand five hundred feet this afternoon.

LKN.discussion_raw:
310
FXUS65 KLKN 120953
AFDLKN

Area Forecast Discussion
National Weather Service Elko NV
Issued by National Weather Service Reno NV
253 AM PDT Sun Apr 12 2026

.KEY MESSAGES...

* Isolated to scattered showers and thunderstorms across portions
  of northern and central Nevada through this evening

* Strong, gusty winds in Central Nevada today

* Precipitation and mountain snow chances increase today through
  Monday along with cooler temperatures

* Snow chances increase tonight into Monday morning

&&


.UPDATE...
Issued at 252 AM PDT Sun Apr 12 2026

No changes were made to the forecast this morning.

&&


.DISCUSSION...
(This afternoon through next Saturday)

WEATHER PATTERN OVERVIEW: Ongoing precipitation with
isolated/scattered thunderstorms this afternoon as the low
pressure system slowly moves over the western CONUS. High
temperatures today around normal in the 50s to 60s, keeping the
precipitation as rain. Highest chances for thunderstorms will be
in northern Nevada and around the I-80 corridor. Strong southerly
winds across central Nevada with speeds up to 15-25 mph, gusts as
high as 35-45 mph. By the afternoon, a brief lull in active
precipitation across the region as the front passes over Nevada.
By tonight, the next wave will begin to impact over western Nevada
moving east with much cooler temperatures, giving more periods of
precipitation and increase of mountain snow as low temperatures
in the valleys drop to just above freezing. Snow is not expected
to reach the valleys as snow levels stay above 8000 feet in the
afternoon, but is expected to drop between 5500-6500 feet by
Sunday morning. Snow levels throughout Sunday will remain around
those levels after sunrise as high temperatures will be just a few
degrees below normal in the 50s. More precipitation and mountain
snow is expected to continue throughout the day with
isolated/scattered thunderstorms picking up again in the
afternoon across the majority of northern and central Nevada.
Strong southerly winds over White Pine County with speeds up to
20-30 mph, gusts 45-55 mph especially along higher passes and
summits. By Sunday night into Monday morning, valley rain will
transition to snow as snow levels drop to below 5000 feet as low
temperatures expected to drop into the 20s. Valley accumulations
expected to be light with the highest amounts up to 0.5 inches (up
to 1 inch near Ely). Higher passes and summits could see up to
1-2 inches (2-3 inches along US-50).

Monday, rain/snow mix is expected in the morning and continue
till the afternoon as the low pressure system moves eastward and
exit Nevada by the end of the day. Temperatures expected to remain
up to 10-15 degrees below normal with highs in the 40s and low
50s. Overnight temperatures expected to stay just below normal in
the 20s.

Tuesday and Wednesday, calmer weather conditions return across
Nevada with gradual warming temperatures reaching back into the
60s and low 70s by Wednesday. Some breezy afternoon winds from the
west expected to increase up to 10-15 mph, gusts as high as 25-30
mph.

Wednesday afternoon and into Thursday morning, models show better
agreement of another round of weather impacting northern Nevada
as a low pressure system along the Canadian border strengthens and
clips the state. The system will push into northern Nevada and
bring increasing chances for more precipitation. It is not
expected to reach into central Nevada and stay north of the US-50
corridor. Slight chance for more mountain snow as this system will
also bring in cooler temperatures. High temperatures Thursday
expected to see northern Nevada in the 40s while central Nevada
cools into the 50s to 60s. Thursday night lows even cooler with
temperatures dropping below normal into the upper teens to 20s.
Overnight precipitation is not expected as the moisture will have
passed over the state by that time.

Friday and into the next weekend, models begin to show
discrepancies after the system passes with some showing an upper
ridge building over the western CONUS while others show a more
zonal flow persisting. The constant with the models is calmer
weather conditions with warming temperatures.

&&


.AVIATION...

Ongoing weather today will keep VCSH and -RA conditions across
all northern terminals today with the chance of seeing some VCTS
and -TSRA this afternoon, but will remain out of the TAFs as probs
are below 30% around the terminals with the exception of KENV.
Greatest chances of thunderstorms occurring this afternoon will be
more across the northern terminals particularly in northeast
Nevada. Strong southerly winds over the central terminals with
speeds up to 15-25 kts, gusts 25-35 kts in KPTH and 25-35 kts,
gusts 40-50 kts in KELY. By the afternoon, the weather is
expected to dissipate with lighter winds (with the exception of
KELY still seeing strong southerly winds up to 15-25 kts, gusts
25-35 kts) until late evening/overnight hours as the next wave
moves into Nevada from the west. -RA conditions expected to reach
over terminals KWMC, KBAM and even KTPH with PROB30 of
transitioning to -SN conditions as cooler temperatures move in.
This next front is also expected to increase winds across all
terminals with speeds up to 15-20 kts, gusts 20-30 kts with the
exception of KELY still seeing strong winds up to 20-25 kts, gusts
30-35 kts.

&&


.LKN Watches/Warnings/Advisories...
Wind Advisory from 7 AM this morning to 5 PM PDT this afternoon
NVZ035.

&&

$$

DISCUSSION...86
AVIATION...97

cools into the 50s to 60s. Thursday night lows even cooler with
temperatures dropping below normal into the upper teens to 20s.
Overnight precipitation is not expected as the moisture will have
passed over the state by that time.

Friday and into the next weekend, models begin to show
discrepancies after the system passes with some showing an upper
ridge building over the western CONUS while others show a more
zonal flow persisting. The constant with the models is calmer
weather conditions with warming temperatures.

&&


.AVIATION...

Ongoing weather today will keep VCSH and -RA conditions across
all northern terminals today with the chance of seeing some VCTS
and -TSRA this afternoon, but will remain out of the TAFs as probs
are below 30% around the terminals with the exception of KENV.
Greatest chances of thunderstorms occurring this afternoon will be
more across the northern terminals particularly in northeast
Nevada. Strong southerly winds over the central terminals with
speeds up to 15-25 kts, gusts 25-35 kts in KPTH and 25-35 kts,
gusts 40-50 kts in KELY. By the afternoon, the weather is
expected to dissipate with lighter winds (with the exception of
KELY still seeing strong southerly winds up to 15-25 kts, gusts
25-35 kts) until late evening/overnight hours as the next wave
moves into Nevada from the west. -RA conditions expected to reach
over terminals KWMC, KBAM and even KTPH with PROB30 of
transitioning to -SN conditions as cooler temperatures move in.
This next front is also expected to increase winds across all
terminals with speeds up to 15-20 kts, gusts 20-30 kts with the
exception of KELY still seeing strong winds up to 20-25 kts, gusts
30-35 kts.

&&


.LKN Watches/Warnings/Advisories...
Wind Advisory from 7 AM this morning to 5 PM PDT this afternoon
NVZ035.

&&

$$

DISCUSSION...86
AVIATION...97
LKN.ai_summary:
Ongoing precipitation with isolated or scattered thunderstorms is expected this afternoon as a low pressure system moves across the western continental United States, with high temperatures remaining near normal in the fifties and sixties, keeping precipitation as rain. The highest potential for these convective events will be situated in northern Nevada and along the Interstate 80 corridor, accompanied by strong southerly winds across central Nevada reaching up to twenty-five miles per hour with gusts up to forty-five miles per hour. As the front passes through the region this afternoon, a brief lull in active precipitation is anticipated. Tonight, the subsequent wave will bring much cooler temperatures and increased periods of precipitation, leading to an increase in mountain snow chances as valley temperatures drop near freezing. Snow levels are expected to drop between five thousand and six thousand five hundred feet by Sunday morning, with accumulations possible up to two to three inches along US-50 passes.

Monday will feature a rain or snow mix as the low pressure system moves eastward and exits Nevada, with temperatures falling ten to fifteen degrees below normal, resulting in highs in the forties and low fifties. Overnight temperatures are forecast to remain just below normal in the twenties. Tuesday and Wednesday will bring calmer conditions with gradual warming, allowing temperatures to reach the sixties and low seventies by Wednesday. Breezy afternoon winds from the west may increase across the region, reaching up to fifteen miles per hour with gusts up to thirty miles per hour. Wednesday afternoon and Thursday morning, a strengthening low pressure system along the Canadian border is expected to impact northern Nevada, increasing chances for precipitation and bringing cooler temperatures, with highs in northern Nevada potentially in the forties while central Nevada remains in the fifties and sixties. Thursday night lows are forecast to be even cooler, dropping below normal into the upper teens to twenties.

VEF.discussion_raw:
516
FXUS65 KVEF 121800
AFDVEF

Area Forecast Discussion
National Weather Service Las Vegas NV
1100 AM PDT Sun Apr 12 2026

.KEY MESSAGES...

 Gusty winds are expected again today, resulting in areas of
  blowing dust, difficult driving conditions, and hazardous boating
  conditions on area lakes. Winds will diminish Monday through
  early week.

* Scattered showers are possible at times today and Monday, with the
  best chances across the Southern Great Basin and higher terrain.

* After a break in active weather, the progressive pattern continues
  as another system may impact the region late in the week.

&&

.DISCUSSION...Through Late this Week.

Across the region, elevated southwest winds were reported in
response to an upper level low moving into Northern California
Coast. Through the early overnight, intermittent gusts over 30 MPH
continued, mainly in the terrain. The area will remain under strong
west to southwesterly flow through today as the low moves inland and
gusty winds are expected to continue. Overnight, the best chance for
gusts over 40 MPH will be in Inyo County through the Western Mojave
Desert into Clark and Lincoln County, especially as increasing mid-
level winds interact with terrain which could cause some downsloping
towards early morning. A Wind Advisory is already in effect for
these areas. After sunrise, better mixing will allow for gusts to 35-
45 MPH to develop areawide. Widespread probabilities 50%+ for gusts
over 40 MPH are noted on ensembles through the afternoon, and
additional Wind Advisories go into effect later this morning in the
Colorado River Valley and Mohave County as stronger winds develop
there. Dangerous boating conditions are expected on area lakes due
to gusty winds and building waves, and in general expect wind
impacts such difficult driving conditions, patchy blowing dust, and
lofted light debris. Winds will diminish this evening, and by Monday
the low is expected to be overhead which will keep winds lighter and
less impactful than today. As ridging builds in early next week
behind the exiting low, lighter winds are expected through early in
the week.

An impressive plume of clouds and precipitation have developed ahead
of the low, with lightning continuing at times overnight. This
shield of precipitation should reach the Sierra in the next few
hours and snow rates will quickly increase as moisture spills into
the Eastern Sierra slopes. Snow will continue in the Sierra through
at least the morning before tapering off through the day. A Winter
Weather Advisory remains in effect above as quick snow accumulations
are expected. There`s a high chance (80% probability) that Aspendell,
CA will see 4 inch+ of new snow, but the chances for snow
accumulations over 8 inches is 10%. Will continue with the Winter
Weather Advisory as it best captures the impacts. Further east,
precipitation will struggle today as most of the moisture will be
intercepted by the Sierra. The best chances today will be in the
terrain with upslope flow interacting with the meager leftover
moisture, as well as the northern fringes of the Southern Great
Basin which will be closest to the trough axis. This afternoon,
short term models are split on how/if scattered showers and an
isolated thunderstorm or two may develop in Lincoln County- with
some models showing precipitation as far south as northeast Clark
County and northern Mohave County. Something to watch as anything
that develops there could continue lightning and sudden gusty winds.
Monday will be even more interesting as a reinforcing piece of
energy digs through the cyclonic flow, which will reinvigorate the
low centered over Southern Nevada by Monday afternoon. Moisture will
still be rather low, but with increased forcing with the piece of
vorticity rounding the base of the low as well as cold temperatures
aloft- scattered showers and thunderstorms may develop in areas
north of the I-15. Models are struggling with how much activity is
able to develop Monday afternoon so low confidence in precipitation
chances as well as impacts such as brief moderate rainfall, small
hail, lightning, and suggest gusty winds.

Improving conditions will return Tuesday and Wednesday as flat high
pressure ridging attempts to build in. After near to slightly below
temperatures through Tuesday, temperatures are expected to warm and
climb back to slightly above normal. It will be a brief break though-
a shortwave moving inland over the PacNW may attempt to drop
southward Thursday across the Great Basin and central Rockies.
Forecast guidance is struggling to resolve this feature which may
remain progressive with only a subtle cooling Thursday and Friday,
or, if the more aggressive solutions pan out, bring sharper cooling
and gusty winds. For now, forecast remains fairly conservative with
modest cooling and breezy conditions on Thursday.

&&

.AVIATION...For Harry Reid...For the 18Z Forecast
Package...Elevated and gusty southwesterly winds will persist
with gusts to around 30KT expected through sunset before
decreasing to 20KT overnight. A brief lull in gusts is expected
Monday morning, though sustained speeds will remain around 10-12KT
before winds ramp up again by early afternoon. Mid-level clouds
will increase through this afternoon, with a 10-15% chance for
high-based showers to move off of the higher terrain into the
Valley late afternoon into early evening. However, most showers
will remain confined to the higher terrain, resulting in
intermittent terrain obscuration. A better chance for showers
(around 20%) to impact the terminal will be at the end of the
period, though timing and coverage remain uncertain. Bands of high
clouds around 10-20kft are expected through Monday morning, with
a swath of cloud cover moving in by early afternoon with ceilings
decreasing to around 6-8kft with shower activity.


For the rest of southern Nevada, northwest Arizona and southeast
California...For the 18Z Forecast Package...A broken band of
showers will move across the region this afternoon and early
evening, with the greatest concentration of showers expected
across the southern Great Basin. Lightning strikes and erratic
gusty winds cannot be ruled out with particularly intense activity
over the higher terrain. Overnight through Monday morning, dry
conditions are expected. Winds will remain southwesterly for the
duration for most locations, gusting to 20-30KT through tonight.
Exceptions include the Owens Valley/KBIH where winds shift to the
northwest tonight with gusts diminishing late evening, and the
Lower Colorado River Valley from KIFP to KEED where gusts diminish
quickly after sunset and speeds drop to around 10KT. VFR
conditions generally prevail, with bands of mid-level and high
clouds from 10-20kft through the period, with lower ceilings to 6-
8kft in the vicinity of any shower activity.

&&

.SPOTTER INFORMATION STATEMENT...Spotters are encouraged to report
any significant weather or impacts according to standard operating
procedures.

&&

$$

DISCUSSION...Nickerson
AVIATION...Phillipson

For more forecast information...see us on our webpage:
https://weather.gov/lasvegas or follow us on Facebook and Twitter

VEF.ai_summary: Elevated southwest winds are driven by an upper level low approaching the Northern California Coast, resulting in intermittent gusts over thirty miles per hour through the day as the low moves inland. These strong west to southwesterly flows interact with terrain, particularly overnight, leading to potential gusts exceeding forty miles per hour in areas like Inyo County and the Western Mojave Desert, with increased mid-level winds causing downsloping effects. Scattered showers are possible today and Monday, with the highest chances occurring across the Southern Great Basin and higher terrain where upslope flow interacts with limited moisture. As the low moves inland, a reinforcing piece of energy is expected to interact with the cyclonic flow on Monday, potentially reinvigorating the low centered over Southern Nevada, which may lead to scattered showers and thunderstorms in areas north of the I-15. Conditions are expected to improve with building high pressure ridging early next week, though a shortwave may attempt to move southward Thursday across the Great Basin and central Rockies, bringing potential for gusty winds.

TWC.discussion_raw:
729
FXUS65 KTWC 120919
AFDTWC

Area Forecast Discussion
National Weather Service Tucson AZ
219 AM MST Sun Apr 12 2026

.SYNOPSIS...High temperatures will be around normal today, then
cooling to below normal Monday and Tuesday. Winds will be breezy
today and Monday with Red Flag conditions expected today. A
weather system will bring a chance for rain mainly north and
east of Tucson Monday afternoon into the overnight hours. After
Tuesday temperatures are expected to be around normal with the
potential for another round of breezy winds Thursday and Friday.

&&

.DISCUSSION...A closed low is currently making its way into
northern California. Today into Monday it will open up and break
into multiple shortwaves, one of which will move through
Arizona Monday. This low already brought breezy winds Saturday
and will increase wind speeds today and Monday. Expect southwest
winds 15-25 mph with gusts up to 40 mph both days. The wind may
also lead to patchy blowing dust for normal trouble areas.
Slightly drier air will also be in place today leading to RH
values below 15 percent across most of southeast Arizona.
Because of this and the strong winds and high fire danger a Red
Flag Warning is in effect from central Pima County eastward from
11 AM to 8 PM.

Monday as the shortwave moves through temperatures cool and
moisture increases marginally. Even though winds will still be
strong the added moisture will eliminate the threat of critical
fire weather conditions. However there will be a chance for
showers beginning Monday afternoon in Cochise County and north
and east of Tucson in the evening and overnight hours. During
the afternoon and early evening also can`t rule out a lightning
strike or two. Best chances will be Monday evening in Pinal
County and northern Graham/Greenlee Counties. Rain amounts will
be very light, generally less than 0.10". Some snow flurries may
also be possible at mountain peaks in Graham and Greenlee
Counties, but no accumulation is expected.

Tuesday the shortwave moves into the Four Corners region as a
Pacific Low moves into the Pacific Northwest and southern
British Columbia. This will be the next system that impacts
southeast Arizona. Models are in better agreement compared to 24
hours ago, bringing the low south through the Great Basin and
into the desert Southwest by Thursday or Friday. Winds will
likely increase again starting Thursday with temperatures
hovering around normal to a couple degrees below normal through
the end of the work week.

&&

.AVIATION Valid though 13/12Z...
SKC this morning, then BKN AOA 15kt ft AGL after 12/18Z through
the end of the period. Sfc winds southerly 5-10 kts this
morning becoming southwesterly 12-20 kts with gusts 25-35 kts
after 12/18Z through 13/04Z. After 13/04Z winds will be
southerly 8-12 kts. Aviation discussion not updated for TAF
amendments.

&&

.FIRE WEATHER...
A Pacific weather system will lead to strong southwesterly winds
today and Monday with speeds 15-25 mph and gusts 30 to 40 mph.
Dryer conditions will be in place today, which combined with the
winds and low fuel moisture will lead to critical fire weather
conditions from central Pima County eastward. A Red Flag Warning
is in place. While winds remain gusty Monday, cooler
temperatures and increased moisture should help mitigate
widespread fire weather concerns. Showers with an embedded
thunderstorm or two will be possible north and east of Tucson
Monday afternoon into the overnight hours. Thereafter, winds
generally 15 mph or less through the middle of next week.
Another Pacific system will likely move through late next week
with strong gusty winds again possible Thursday and Friday.

&&

.TWC WATCHES/WARNINGS/ADVISORIES...
Red Flag Warning from 11 AM this morning to 8 PM MST this
evening for AZZ150>152.

&&

$$

Hardin

Visit us on Facebook, X, YouTube, and at weather.gov/Tucson

TWC.ai_summary:
A closed low is advancing into northern California, initiating a series of shortwave troughs that will impact the region. This system brings strong southwesterly winds with gusts up to forty miles per hour for today and Monday, accompanied by drier air conditions across much of southeast Arizona. As the shortwave moves through Arizona on Monday, temperatures cool slightly while moisture increases marginally. Showers with embedded thunderstorms are possible in Cochise County and areas north and east of Tucson during the afternoon and overnight hours of Monday.

Tuesday, a Pacific Low shifts into the Pacific Northwest, moving south through the Great Basin and into the desert Southwest by Thursday or Friday. Winds are expected to increase again starting Thursday, with temperatures remaining near normal to slightly below normal through the end of the work week.

PSR.discussion_raw:
294
FXUS65 KPSR 121711
AFDPSR

Area Forecast Discussion
National Weather Service Phoenix AZ
1011 AM MST Sun Apr 12 2026

.UPDATE...18Z Aviation Discussion.

&&

.KEY MESSAGES...

- A slow moving weather system will bring cooler temperatures and
  breezy to locally windy conditions through Monday as well as
  late Monday rain chances.

- Weak high pressure will settle back into the region during the
  middle of the week leading to drier and slightly warmer
  conditions.

&&

.SHORT TERM /TODAY THROUGH MONDAY/...
Large scale troughing continues to impact much of the Western U.S.
with the second in a series of three weather systems currently
moving into northern California. The main impact for the Desert
Southwest today will be another day of breezy to windy conditions
with afternoon/early evening wind gusts commonly reaching 20-30
mph. Drier air remains positioned across the region as the
weather system is still too far removed to the northwest. Guidance
also shows the second disturbance quickly weakening today as it
moves eastward into the Great Basin, while a third piece of energy
moves southeastward into the base of the trough tonight. Aside
from the increased winds today, temperatures are also expected to
at most reach into the normal range with highs mostly in the lower
80s across the lower deserts. Today should be our coolest day
since around March 11th.

By Monday afternoon, a modest shortwave trough is forecast to move
into southern California, a combination of the second and third
pieces of energy. Like today, Monday will again be another breezy
to windy day, but with the PV energy moving through the region
later in the day it should result in some scattered shower
activity. The lack of decent moisture will still be a limiting
factor for rain chances, but models have at least maintained or
even slightly increased chances with the latest run. Forecast
soundings by Monday evening show a shallow moist layer centered
around 10K feet, but little moisture above and below this layer.
Considerable dry air in the boundary layer is expected to limit
measurable rainfall to mostly higher terrain areas, but even good
a portion of the lower deserts should receive some trace amounts
from late Monday afternoon through Monday night. PoPs have
increased slightly from the previous forecast package with 10-15%
chances over southeast California and southwest Arizona, 20-30%
within Phoenix, to as high as 40-50% over portions of the high
terrain.

&&

.LONG TERM /TUESDAY THROUGH SATURDAY/...
The weather disturbance will begin exit to the east during the
first half of Tuesday with any limited lingering rain chances
across far eastern Arizona. Drier air will begin to move in from
the west on Tuesday allowing for skies to become clear to mostly
clear. Temperatures will however continue to be below normal with
the upper 70s to lower 80s on Monday carrying over into Tuesday.

Weak upper level ridging is then expected to move through the
region on Wednesday helping to boost daytime highs back into the
mid 80s, or right around normal. The weather pattern for the rest
of the week should stay relatively quiet, but a mostly dry
Pacific trough is likely to track by just to our north late in
the week. Ensemble guidance is still a bit uncertain with this
system, but for now has it tracking across the Four Corners area.
It is quite unlikely it will result in any rain chances, but we
very well could get another day or two of breezy conditions
around Thursday or Friday. The NBM has also responded by dropping
highs on Friday back into the lower 80s, but this will not last
long as incoming ridging favored for next weekend should
eventually push readings back to above normal.

&&

.AVIATION...Updated at 1715Z.

South Central Arizona including KPHX, KIWA, KSDL, and KDVT:
The main aviation concerns over the next 24 hrs will be another
period of gusty winds this afternoon and evening. Winds will
become dominate from the west with gusts up to 20-25 kts into the
early evening. Winds will drop below 10kts tonight and the wind
shift should be later than normal to the southeast, but
confidence is low to the extent of this diurnal shift tonight.
High clouds will be abundant through the period with some SCT
clouds as low as 9k feet. Virga and a few sprinkles will be
possible overnight and into Monday morning.

Southeast California/Southwest Arizona including KIPL and KBLH:
The main aviation concerns at both TAF sites will be gusty winds,
especially this afternoon and evening, with gusts 20-30 kts. Winds
will remain westerly at KIPL and south to southwesterly at KBLH
through the TAF period. SCT-BKN high clouds will continue to be
common through the period.

&&

.FIRE WEATHER...
An unsettled weather pattern will continue through the first part
of this week resulting in near to below normal temperatures and
slightly elevated humidities. MinRHs through Monday will mostly
average 20-25% with overnight recoveries of 40-70%. Winds will
somewhat follow diurnal patterns, but with more of a predominant
southwesterly direction and gusty afternoon and evening winds of
25-35 mph over much of the area. Chances for light showers will be
possible later Monday and Monday night, but CWR will remain below
10%. Weak high pressure will then settle back over the region
during the latter half of the week leading to drier conditions
and temperatures staying close to normal.

&&

AZ...None.
CA...None.

LONG TERM...Kuhlman

PSR.synoptic_extract:
294
FXUS65 KPSR 121711
AFDPSR

Area Forecast Discussion
National Weather Service Phoenix AZ
1011 AM MST Sun Apr 12 2026

.UPDATE...18Z Aviation Discussion.

&&

.KEY MESSAGES...

- A slow moving weather system will bring cooler temperatures and
  breezy to locally windy conditions through Monday as well as
  late Monday rain chances.

- Weak high pressure will settle back into the region during the
  middle of the week leading to drier and slightly warmer
  conditions.

&&

.SHORT TERM /TODAY THROUGH MONDAY/...
Large scale troughing continues to impact much of the Western U.S.
with the second in a series of three weather systems currently
moving into northern California. The main impact for the Desert
Southwest today will be another day of breezy to windy conditions
with afternoon/early evening wind gusts commonly reaching 20-30
mph. Drier air remains positioned across the region as the
weather system is still too far removed to the northwest. Guidance
also shows the second disturbance quickly weakening today as it
moves eastward into the Great Basin, while a third piece of energy
moves southeastward into the base of the trough tonight. Aside
from the increased winds today, temperatures are also expected to
at most reach into the normal range with highs mostly in the lower
80s across the lower deserts. Today should be our coolest day
since around March 11th.

By Monday afternoon, a modest shortwave trough is forecast to move
into southern California, a combination of the second and third
pieces of energy. Like today, Monday will again be another breezy
to windy day, but with the PV energy moving through the region
later in the day it should result in some scattered shower
activity. The lack of decent moisture will still be a limiting
factor for rain chances, but models have at least maintained or
even slightly increased chances with the latest run. Forecast
soundings by Monday evening show a shallow moist layer centered
around 10K feet, but little moisture above and below this layer.
Considerable dry air in the boundary layer is expected to limit
measurable rainfall to mostly higher terrain areas, but even good
a portion of the lower deserts should receive some trace amounts
from late Monday afternoon through Monday night. PoPs have
increased slightly from the previous forecast package with 10-15%
chances over southeast California and southwest Arizona, 20-30%
within Phoenix, to as high as 40-50% over portions of the high
terrain.

&&

.LONG TERM /TUESDAY THROUGH SATURDAY/...
The weather disturbance will begin exit to the east during the
first half of Tuesday with any limited lingering rain chances
across far eastern Arizona. Drier air will begin to move in from
the west on Tuesday allowing for skies to become clear to mostly
clear. Temperatures will however continue to be below normal with
the upper 70s to lower 80s on Monday carrying over into Tuesday.

Weak upper level ridging is then expected to move through the
region on Wednesday helping to boost daytime highs back into the
mid 80s, or right around normal. The weather pattern for the rest
of the week should stay relatively quiet, but a mostly dry
Pacific trough is likely to track by just to our north late in
the week. Ensemble guidance is still a bit uncertain with this
system, but for now has it tracking across the Four Corners area.
It is quite unlikely it will result in any rain chances, but we
very well could get another day or two of breezy conditions
around Thursday or Friday. The NBM has also responded by dropping
highs on Friday back into the lower 80s, but this will not last
long as incoming ridging favored for next weekend should
eventually push readings back to above normal.

&&

.AVIATION...Updated at 1715Z.

South Central Arizona including KPHX, KIWA, KSDL, and KDVT:
The main aviation concerns over the next 24 hrs will be another
period of gusty winds this afternoon and evening. Winds will
become dominate from the west with gusts up to 20-25 kts into the
early evening. Winds will drop below 10kts tonight and the wind
shift should be later than normal to the southeast, but
confidence is low to the extent of this diurnal shift tonight.
High clouds will be abundant through the period with some SCT
clouds as low as 9k feet. Virga and a few sprinkles will be
possible overnight and into Monday morning.

Southeast California/Southwest Arizona including KIPL and KBLH:
The main aviation concerns at both TAF sites will be gusty winds,
especially this afternoon and evening, with gusts 20-30 kts. Winds
will remain westerly at KIPL and south to southwesterly at KBLH
through the TAF period. SCT-BKN high clouds will continue to be
common through the period.

&&

.FIRE WEATHER...
An unsettled weather pattern will continue through the first part
of this week resulting in near to below normal temperatures and
slightly elevated humidities. MinRHs through Monday will mostly
average 20-25% with overnight recoveries of 40-70%. Winds will
somewhat follow diurnal patterns, but with more of a predominant
southwesterly direction and gusty afternoon and evening winds of
25-35 mph over much of the area. Chances for light showers will be
possible later Monday and Monday night, but CWR will remain below
10%. Weak high pressure will then settle back over the region
during the latter half of the week leading to drier conditions
and temperatures staying close to normal.

&&

.PSR WATCHES/WARNINGS/ADVISORIES...
AZ...None.
CA...None.
&&

$$

SHORT TERM...Kuhlman
LONG TERM...Kuhlman
AVIATION...Frieders
FIRE WEATHER...Kuhlman
PSR.ai_summary:
A slow-moving weather system is influencing the region, bringing cooler temperatures and breezy to windy conditions through Monday, with chances for rain later in the day on Monday. Large scale troughing continues to impact the Western U.S., with subsequent disturbances moving eastward into the Great Basin and southeastward into the base of a trough tonight. Today will feature increased winds, with afternoon and evening gusts reaching twenty to thirty miles per hour, and temperatures expected to remain near normal, with highs in the lower eighties across the lower deserts. By Monday afternoon, a modest shortwave trough is forecast to move into southern California, bringing further breezy conditions and scattered shower activity, although moisture limitations will restrict measurable rainfall.

Looking ahead to the longer term, the weather disturbance is expected to exit to the east during the first half of Tuesday, allowing drier air to advance from the west and leading to clearer skies. Weak upper level ridging is anticipated to move through on Wednesday, helping daytime highs return to the mid eighties or near normal. The pattern for the remainder of the week should remain relatively quiet, though breezy conditions could persist around Thursday or Friday. A mostly dry Pacific trough is likely to track by late in the week, and while some moisture may be present overnight, chances for significant rainfall are low.

FGZ.discussion_raw:
379
FXUS65 KFGZ 121759
AFDFGZ

Area Forecast Discussion
National Weather Service Flagstaff AZ
1059 AM MST Sun Apr 12 2026

.SYNOPSIS...Windy and cooler weather will continue into the early
part of next week. The next round of showers is expected Monday
afternoon into Tuesday. Some light snow could fall above 6000 feet
Monday night into Tuesday morning.

&&

.DISCUSSION...Over the next few days, a trough currently located
over California will make its way across the Desert Southwest. For
today, it will be dry but very windy as the polar jet wraps around
the southern periphery of this trough and moves across Arizona.
Winds will translate to the surface by mid morning reaching speeds
out of the southwest at 20-30 mph, gusting 40-45 mph. A Wind
Advisory has been issued for most of the area starting at 8AM
(9AM Navajo Nation) and ending at 8PM (9PM Navajo Nation). Cooler
air will start filtering into the area with daytime highs around
seasonal averages.

By Monday the trough begins to move across the state. The polar
jet still remains over us but will start to move a little further
to the east which will again mean high winds at the surface but
maybe not as intense across the western half of the state as on
Sunday. Will watch and see if another Wind Advisory needs to be
issued or not, but if one is the eastern zones will likely be the
favored area. Moisture associated with this trough moves in by
mid to late morning with shower chances over the western half of
the CWA during early afternoon, and across the whole CWA by Monday
evening. Colder air continues to filter in with highs about 5
degrees below average. By Monday night into Tuesday enough cold
air moves in to drop snow levels potentially as low as 6000ft.
Moisture values have ticked up a little over the latest runs and
its possible that areas above 6500ft could see up to an inch of
accumulation by Tuesday morning. Slick roads may be a possibility
if you`re traveling overnight during that time period, but any
snow accumulations will likely be short lived and be gone by
midday Tuesday. Overall QPF totals by midday Tuesday range from a
few hundredths over the high deserts to a quarter or third of an
inch over the higher elevations. Tuesday`s max temperatures will
be the coldest we`ve seen in a little while, with highs about 10
degrees below average.

By midday to late Tuesday evening the trough will exit off to the
east and things dry out for Wednesday and Thursday as a zonal flow
aloft takes over. Temperatures recover back towards seasonal
averages. By Friday another trough could potentially impact the
Desert Southwest, though how far south it comes and how much
moisture is associated with it remains to be seen. We will keep an
eye on it to see if anything materializes.

&&

.AVIATION...Sunday 12/18Z through Monday 13/18Z...VFR conditions.
Winds today are SW 20-30 kts gusting 35-45 kts. Winds decrease
after 03Z, but stay elevated from the S-SW at 10-20kts through the
night. Winds begin to ramp up after 16Z, becoming SW 20-30 kts
gusting 35-40 kts.

OUTLOOK...Monday 13/18Z through Wednesday 15/18Z...MVFR-IFR
conditions possible as -SHRA spreads after 18Z Monday. Transition
to SHSN after 00-06Z Tuesday for sites above 6500 ft. SCT
SHRA/SHSN continue through 00Z Wednesday, then VFR thereafter.
Winds are SW 20-30 kts gusting 35-40kts on Monday, then W-NW 10-20
kts gusting to 30 kts on Tuesday.

&&

.FIRE WEATHER...Today and Monday...Dry and windy during the day,
then isolated showers possible in far NW AZ late this evening
through Monday morning. A 40-70% chance of showers spreads across
most of northern AZ Monday afternoon lasting into Tuesday morning.
Snow levels drop as low as 6000 ft on Monday night, with
accumulations up to an inch possible above 6500-7000 ft by Tuesday
morning.  Winds are SW 20-30 mph with gusts 35-45 mph each day.
Minimum RH is 15-30% on Sunday, then 20-50% on Monday.

Tuesday through Thursday...Below normal temperatures with a
lingering 20-30% chance of showers on Tuesday, then dry with near
normal temperatures on Wednesday and Thursday. Winds SW-W 5-15 mph
Tuesday morning, switching to the W-NW 5-15 mph with gusts 20-25 mph
by Tuesday afternoon. On Wednesday winds SW-W 5-15 mph, becoming SW
10-20 mph gusting 25-35 mph on Thursday. Minimum RH is 20-40% on
Tuesday, then 10-25% Wednesday and Thursday.

&&

.FGZ WATCHES/WARNINGS/ADVISORIES...
Wind Advisory until 8 PM MST /9 PM MDT/ this evening for AZZ004-
006>017-039-040.

&&

$$

PUBLIC...Bernhart
AVIATION...Lewandowski
FIRE WEATHER...Bernhart

For Northern Arizona weather information visit
weather.gov/flagstaff

FGZ.ai_summary:
A trough currently positioned over California is expected to advance across the Desert Southwest, initiating a period of strong winds as the polar jet wraps around its southern periphery and moves through Arizona. Surface winds will be from the southwest, reaching twenty to thirty miles per hour with gusts up to forty to forty-five miles per hour during the morning hours. Cooler air will begin to filter into the region, bringing daytime highs near seasonal averages.

As Monday arrives, the trough continues its eastward progression, maintaining high surface winds, though potentially less intense across the western portions of the state than on Sunday. Moisture associated with this system moves in by mid to late morning, leading to shower chances over the western half of the Colorado Plateau during the early afternoon and extending across the entire region by Monday evening. Colder air continues to advance, with temperatures dropping about five degrees below average. By Monday night into Tuesday morning, cold air intrusion is expected to cause snow levels to drop potentially as low as six thousand feet, with accumulations possible above six thousand five hundred feet by Tuesday morning. The trough will then exit to the east by midday to late Tuesday evening, allowing for a transition to a zonal flow and recovery toward seasonal average temperatures through Wednesday and Thursday.

TFX.discussion_raw:
951
FXUS65 KTFX 121836
AFDTFX

Area Forecast Discussion
National Weather Service Great Falls MT
1236 PM MDT Sun Apr 12 2026

.KEY MESSAGES...

 - Another round of showers and thunderstorms this afternoon with
   additional precipitation tonight into Monday.

 - Breezy Tuesday and Wednesday.

 - Much cooler precipitation producing system Wednesday into
   Thursday, with lower elevation snow by Thursday morning.

&&

.DISCUSSION...

 - Meteorological Overview:

A south to southwest flow aloft remain in place associated with a
weakening upper level trough near northern CA this afternoon. An
initial wave ejecting from this trough is moving across the region
now, aiding in the development of showers and thunderstorms near
the ID border. This convective activity moves north and
northeastward through the remainder of the afternoon and early
evening. Gusty winds and lightning across Southwest and Central
Montana will be the primary concern today.

An additional wave from the weakening troughing near CA lifts toward
the region tonight resulting in a stratiform type precipitation band
tonight into Monday. Some light snow near and above pass level is
forecast, but impacts look minimal at this time. Additional showery
activity looks to develop Monday afternoon in areas that are able to
eek out some sunshine and develop weak instability.

Zonal flow aloft develops Tuesday into Wednesday ahead of troughing
shifting southeastward from the Gulf of Alaska toward the Pacific
NW. Breezy west to southwest winds develop as a result. While a
brief stretch of stronger wind cannot be ruled out, this just looks
to be a breezy timeframe at this point.

The upper level system approaching the Pacific NW Wednesday shifts
eastward and over the Northern Rockies Wednesday evening into
Thursday. Much cooler air works in with a cold front, bringing snow
levels down to lower elevations. There is still some uncertainty
with respect to overall amounts with this system (Largely related to
the storm track), but at least brief light snow at lower elevations
is a reasonable expectation at this time by Thursday morning.

Cluster guidance favors brief ridging developing Friday into the
weekend, which would favor a drier stretch, with temperatures slowly
trending warmer. By early next week approximately 90% of cluster
guidance members have some sort of troughing returning across the
west. -AM

 - Forecast Confidence & Scenarios:

Thunderstorms today and precipitation through Monday:

Inverted-V soundings across Southwest Montana for the remainder of
the afternoon suggest gusty outflow winds will be the main concern
today. The risk for severe thunderstorms today across Southwest
Montana is 5% within 25 miles of a given point.

Heading into tonight showery activity diminishes, with a banded type
of stratiform precipitation taking over. The overall areal extent of
this band appears to be narrow, which has resulted in probability of
precipitation decreasing in many areas for tonight as confidence in
its placement has increased. Given the cooler air aloft going into
Monday, areas that clear out sufficiently Monday morning and
afternoon will see at least a low-probabiltiy for afternoon showers.
All precipitation diminishes by Monday night.


Winds Tuesday into Wednesday:

Ridgetop stability has weakened slightly over the timeframe in which
the strongest zonal winds aloft are currently timed. As such, the
probability for a 55 mph gust along the Rocky Mountain Front Tuesday
and Wednesday has decreased ever so slightly. Further east in Cut
Bank the probability for a 55 mph gust is between 40 and 50% over
this timeframe.


Precipitation Wednesday into Thursday:

Volatility is still present, but confidence remains high in a trend
cooler, with at least light lower elevation snow by Thursday morning
across the region. Areas most favored for impactful snow in terrain
are south of I-90 and east of I-15. The probability for 9 inches of
snow in these mountains for this event is greater than 50%. -AM

&&

.AVIATION...
12/18Z TAF Period

Scattered to widespread showers and isolated thunderstorms are
expected to develop across Southwest MT this afternoon. These
showers/thunderstorms will bring MVFR conditions at times, with
gusty erratic winds possible with a few stronger storms. This band
of showers will lift north throughout the afternoon through
Monday morning, switching to stratiform rain mainly in North-
Central MT. There remains some uncertainty in how far north this
band of rain lifts, which gives low confidence in end timing for
rain at terminals in North- Central MT. MVFR ceilings will be
persistent through the TAF period across North-Central MT, with
IFR clouds developing Monday morning. Mountain obscuration will
be possible at times due to precipitation and low ceilings.
Morning fog at KCTB is currently dissipating, but IFR ceilings
may linger for another hour or two. -Wilson

&&

.PRELIMINARY POINT TEMPS/POPS...
GTF  38  58  39  59 /  70  50  20   0
CTB  32  58  37  54 /  30  30  10   0
HLN  36  57  35  59 /  80  40  20   0
BZN  31  56  31  60 /  30  50  40  10
WYS  26  44  26  47 /  60  50  50  20
DLN  32  52  30  56 /  40  40  10   0
HVR  36  57  37  63 /  60  60  30   0
LWT  35  56  34  59 /  90  30  40  10

&&

.TFX WATCHES/WARNINGS/ADVISORIES...
None.
&&

$$
http://www.weather.gov/greatfalls

TFX.ai_summary:
A south to southwest flow aloft is in place, associated with a weakening upper level trough near northern California, which initiates a wave moving across the region that aids in the development of showers and thunderstorms near the Idaho border this afternoon, moving north and northeastward through the afternoon and early evening. An additional wave from the weakening trough lifts toward the region tonight into Monday, resulting in a stratiform precipitation band for tonight into Monday, with some light snow possible at higher elevations. Showery activity is expected to develop again Monday afternoon in areas with weak instability.

Zonal flow aloft develops Tuesday into Wednesday ahead of troughing shifting southeastward from the Gulf of Alaska toward the Pacific Northwest, leading to breezy west to southwest winds. The upper level system approaches the Pacific Northwest Wednesday evening and shifts eastward over the Northern Rockies into Thursday, where cooler air interacts with a cold front, bringing snow levels down to lower elevations by Thursday morning. This system suggests a trend toward ridging developing Friday into the weekend, which favors drier conditions as temperatures slowly warm.

GGW.discussion_raw:
605
FXUS65 KGGW 120754
AFDGGW

Area Forecast Discussion
National Weather Service Glasgow MT
154 AM MDT Sun Apr 12 2026

.KEY MESSAGES...

- Seasonable temperatures and widespread rain showers are expected
  tonight and Monday.

- Below normal temperatures with significant mixed precipitation
  is becoming more likely for Thursday and Friday.

&&

.DISCUSSION...

WEATHER PATTERN OVERVIEW:

Upper low off the northern California coast will lift through the
Great Basin through the early part of the week. This will continue
to push Pacific moisture into the state, with another round of
showers possible tonight into Monday. Some colder air will filter
south from Canada, for a little light snow possible along the
Canadian border. At the same time, thunderstorms are possible in
the southwest ahead of the California low.

The next upper trough moves into the northern Rockies on Wednesday
and into the plains through Friday. This will lead to another
round of showers, and with colder air with the trough, snow is
possible across much of the region Wednesday night into Thursday,
particularly for elevated areas. Chances for at least 2" of snow
at this time are 20-40%.

FORECAST CONFIDENCE & DEVIATIONS:

Minor changes to the day 1 forecast with a slight increase in
temperatures and winds for today.

Area Forecast Discussion
National Weather Service Glasgow MT
154 AM MDT Sun Apr 12 2026

.KEY MESSAGES...

- Seasonable temperatures and widespread rain showers are expected
  tonight and Monday.

- Below normal temperatures with significant mixed precipitation
  is becoming more likely for Thursday and Friday.

&&

.DISCUSSION...

WEATHER PATTERN OVERVIEW:

Upper low off the northern California coast will lift through the
Great Basin through the early part of the week. This will continue
to push Pacific moisture into the state, with another round of
showers possible tonight into Monday. Some colder air will filter
south from Canada, for a little light snow possible along the
Canadian border. At the same time, thunderstorms are possible in
the southwest ahead of the California low.

The next upper trough moves into the northern Rockies on Wednesday
and into the plains through Friday. This will lead to another
round of showers, and with colder air with the trough, snow is
possible across much of the region Wednesday night into Thursday,
particularly for elevated areas. Chances for at least 2" of snow
at this time are 20-40%.

FORECAST CONFIDENCE & DEVIATIONS:

Minor changes to the day 1 forecast with a slight increase in
temperatures and winds for today.

While there is moderate to high confidence in two systems
producing wetting precipitation, confidence is generally low for
widespread accumulating snow to occur later in the week.

tonight and linger into Monday. Brief MVFR conditions are possible
with showers.

None.

Cold air from Canada may filter southward, bringing a possibility of light snow along the Canadian border. Thunderstorms are anticipated in the southwest ahead of the California low. A subsequent upper trough is forecast to move into the northern Rockies on Wednesday and subsequently into the plains through Friday. This feature will bring further showers, and the associated colder air will increase the possibility of snow across much of the region Wednesday night into Thursday, with chances for at least two inches of snow being twenty to forty percent.

BYZ.discussion_raw:
544
FXUS65 KBYZ 121857
AFDBYZ

Area Forecast Discussion
National Weather Service Billings MT
1257 PM MDT Sun Apr 12 2026

.KEY MESSAGES...

- Scattered showers and thunderstorms this afternoon and evening
  will be capable of producing gusty winds 30-50 mph.

- Gusty west to southwest winds (40 to 50 mph) over the western
  foothills late tonight through Monday.

- Another period of scattered rain showers and isolated
  thunderstorms, producing gusty winds, Monday afternoon and
  evening. Best chances for rain will be east of Billings.

- Stronger and colder storm system late Wednesday into Friday will
  bring significantly colder temperatures, high chances (50-90%)
  for precipitation (including low elevation snow Thursday night)
  and the potential for heavy mountain snow.

&&

.DISCUSSION...

Rest of Today through Saturday...

This afternoon and tonight...Synoptic Scale low off the NorCal
coast continues spin waves of energy out of the Great Basin and
across our area. Another such wave will move through this
afternoon through late tonight producing showers and isolated
thunderstorms as it crosses. Moisture profiles improved over the
past 24 hours due to the lack of wind today and the scattered
convection yesterday. As a result there will be a better chance to
get precipitation to the ground with todays activity compared to
yesterday when most areas saw gusty winds but little
precipitation. That said there is still the potential for wind
gusts in the 30 to 50 mph range near any storms, with a few
stronger gusts possible (5% chance) mainly from Billings west into
the foothills. Areas that get under a thunderstorm could see a
tenth or two of precipitation, but most areas will see less than
that with showers through tonight.

Monday...Another area of vorticity spins northeast into central
Idaho late tonight. This feature will angle winds down the
Paradise Valley as well as lowering pressures lee of the divide.
These processes will combine to increase winds over the western
foothills by early morning through most of the day Monday, with
peak wind speeds likely occuring around noon. NBM probabilities
for gusts 50+ mph are around 70 percent for Livingston, 50 percent
for Big Timber and 40 percent for Harlowton. Probabilities for
60+ mph are well short of needing wind highlites at about 35
percent for Livingston, and in the single digits along the US-191
corridor from Big Timber to Harlowton.

Downslope wind gradient will hold precipitation chances down in
the 15 to 25 percent range from Billings west on Monday
afternoon/evening, but east of Billings precipitation chances will
be higher in the 30 to 50% range as the aforementioned disturbance
crosses the area. Once again an isolated thunderstorm with gusty
winds will be possible just about everywhere Monday
afternoon/evening, with the best chances for a stronger wind gust
being over SE Montana. High temperatures Monday will continue a
downward slide into the 55-65 degree range.

Tuesday...NorCal low pressure system kicks east Monday night and
Tuesday but splits as it does so. There will be some energy
crossing our area, but the majority heads into the Four Corners
area. Downslope winds over us will limit precipitation chances for
us Tuesday into Wednesday, with best chances remaining in the
mountains. Highs Tuesday rebound in some areas with most locations
expecting mid-60s.

Wednesday-Friday...After a brief break, precipitation chances
ramp up quickly late Wednesday into Thursday with another strong
low pressure system dropping southeast from southern B.C. into
Idaho and W. Montana. This energy also splits but does so closer
to our area and spreads a swath of decent precipitation potential
across the western 2/3 of the forecast area through Thursday
night. Probabilities for over a quarter inch of precipitation Wed
night through early Friday are over 60% west of a Roundup to Lame
Deer line and south across Sheridan county.

Snow chances return...This system is of a colder nature than the
early week California system. Snow levels around 7000 ft on
Wednesday drop to 4500 feet by Thursday morning and down to 3500
feet by Thursday evening. So, this system will bring some snow to
the lower elevations, especially Thursday night into Friday
morning.Probabilities for 3+ inches of snow Thursday night-Friday
morning range from around 50% for Livingston, Big Timber and
Sheridan, to around 25% for Billings and Hardin. Chances further
north and east taper off to probabilities in the teens for
Roundup, Miles City, Baker, and Broadus vicinities. Mountain
locations could be in for a period of heavy snowfall with this
system. This time of year the above probabilities are mainly for
accumulations on grass and elevated surfaces. Probabilities for
over a foot of snowfall are 60% for the Crazy mountains, up to 90%
for the Beartooth/Absaroka mountains, and over 50% for the
Bighorn mountains.

Highs in the 60s (maybe some lower 70s SE Montana) Wednesday drop
into the 40s and 50s Thursday, with a few upper 30s possible for
the western foothills (Livingston/Harlowton/Big Timber). Fridays
highs stay cool in the 35 to 45 degree range with plenty of clouds
and areas of precipitation. There is still a lot of time for
changes to occur with this system. If you have outdoor or travel
plans mid to late week, keep up with the forecast for the latest
details and plan accordingly.

Next Weekend...Ridging builds in for next weekend for drier and
warmer conditions. Temperatures forecast in the 45-55 range on
Saturday, climbing into the 55-65 range for Sunday. Chambers

.AVIATION...

A round of scattered showers and isolated thunderstorms will move
through the region this afternoon and evening. Expect gusty and
erratic winds of 25 to 40 kts with any thunderstorms and
localized MVFR conditions under the heaviest showers. Isolated
showers may linger east of KBIL into Monday morning. MVFR ceilings
move in over the Dakota border, including near KMLS and KBHK,
Monday morning. Archer

&&
